results.title_inferred: Remedy to Beautify the Skin
results.original_text:
REMEDY TO BEAUTIFY THE SKIN

Meal-of-Alabaster
Meal-of-Natron
Sea-salt
Honey
results.translation: A remedy to beautify the skin: Meal of alabaster [finely ground alabaster powder], meal of natron [ground natron], sea-salt, honey. [Mix together and apply to the skin.]

entities_mentioned.name: Meal-of-Alabaster
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Finely ground calcium carbonate or calcium sulfate (alabaster/calcite)
entities_mentioned.notes: Alabaster in ancient Egypt typically refers to calcite (Egyptian alabaster), not the gypsum-based alabaster of later periods. Ground into a fine powder for cosmetic use as an exfoliant or skin-smoothing agent.

entities_mentioned.name: Meal-of-Natron
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sodium carbonate / sodium bicarbonate (natron)
entities_mentioned.notes: Natron is a naturally occurring salt mixture composed chiefly of sodium carbonate decahydrate with sodium bicarbonate, sodium chloride, and sodium sulfate. Widely used in ancient Egypt for cleansing and purification.

entities_mentioned.name: Sea-salt
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sodium chloride (NaCl)
entities_mentioned.notes: Common sea salt; used here likely as a mild abrasive and preservative component in the cosmetic preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: Honey
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Honey (Apis mellifera product)
entities_mentioned.notes: Honey appears frequently in Ebers Papyrus remedies as a binding agent, humectant, and antimicrobial ingredient. Here it likely serves as a binder and skin-conditioning agent.

results.translator_notes: The source text appears incomplete — no quantities, preparation method, or application instructions are given, suggesting either a lacuna in the original papyrus or that this entry was truncated in the 1930 Bryan translation. The page reference 'COSMETICS 161' is a section/page header from the printed edition and not part of the original Egyptian text. The remedy is a cosmetic paste or scrub formula consistent with other ancient Egyptian skin-care preparations combining abrasives, alkaline salts, and a moisturising binder.
results._label: Remedy To Beautify The Skin

uadu-plant-of-the-Fields

results.translation: A remedy to drive away sweaty feet in a person: [Take] the uadu-plant of the Fields [and] fel from the Canal. Warm in oil and smear both feet therewith.

entities_mentioned.name: uadu-plant-of-the-Fields
entities_mentioned.notes: An Egyptian plant designated 'uadu' (also rendered 'wad' or 'waad' in some transliterations), specified as the field variety. Precise botanical identification remains uncertain; possibly a sedge or rush species common to Egyptian agricultural land.

entities_mentioned.name: Fel-from-the-Canal
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Fel' may refer to a mineral deposit, algal mat, sediment, or organic matter collected from canal water. The term is ambiguous in the source and may reflect an OCR or transliteration artifact; could potentially be 'silt' or a specific plant gathered from canal banks.

entities_mentioned.name: Oil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely vegetable oil, possibly castor oil (Ricinus communis) or moringa oil (Moringa oleifera), both common in ancient Egyptian medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: Oil is used as the carrier/vehicle for warming and topical application to the feet. The specific oil type is not identified in this passage.

results.translator_notes: The segment is followed by a chapter heading ('CHAPTER XXIV – DOMESTIC HINTS') and editorial commentary by Bryan, which has been excluded from the remedy translation as it constitutes the translator's own framing rather than source text. The preparation method is simple: warm the ingredients in oil and apply topically to both feet. The condition treated is hyperhidrosis (excessive sweating) of the feet.

results.title_inferred: Remedy to Stop the Crying of a Child
results.original_text:
REMEDY TO STOP THE CRYING OF A CHILD

Pods-of-the-Poppy-plant (Opium)
Fly-dirt-which-is-on-the-Wall
results.translation: A remedy to stop the crying of a child: [administer] pods of the poppy plant [opium] and fly-dirt [fly specks] found on the wall.

entities_mentioned.name: Pods-of-the-Poppy-plant (Opium)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Papaver somniferum (opium poppy)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Bryan translation explicitly glosses this ingredient as opium. Opium derived from poppy pods was used in ancient Egypt as a sedative and analgesic, and its use to quiet crying children is well attested in the Ebers Papyrus. The active alkaloids (morphine, codeine) would produce genuine sedative effects, though the practice is of course highly dangerous by modern standards.

entities_mentioned.name: Fly-dirt-which-is-on-the-Wall
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fly excrement / fly specks
entities_mentioned.notes: This refers to the dark specks left by flies on walls. It appears as a component in several Ebers Papyrus remedies. Its inclusion likely reflects magical or sympathetic thinking rather than pharmacological efficacy. Some scholars suggest it may have functioned as a vehicle or filler ingredient, or carried ritual significance.

results.translator_notes: The passage is brief and lacks explicit preparation or dosage instructions in this segment, suggesting that application method may have been described elsewhere in the papyrus or was considered understood by practitioners. The use of opium to sedate children is consistent with other ancient medical traditions but represents a serious hazard by modern standards. The fly excrement ingredient is characteristic of the Egyptian materia medica's inclusion of waste and animal-derived substances, often interpreted as having apotropaic or magical functions alongside physical ones.
results._label: Remedy To Stop The Crying Of A Child

results.title_inferred: Remedy to Stop the Crying of a Child
results.original_text:
REMEDY TO STOP THE CRYING OF A CHILD

(NEW STYLE)
Powder-which-contains-Opium
Fly-dirt-which-is-on-the-Dummy
results.translation: A remedy to stop the crying of a child (new style [i.e., a revised or updated formulation]): A powder containing opium; fly excrement found on the [infant's] pacifier [dummy].

entities_mentioned.name: Powder-which-contains-Opium
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Opium (dried latex of Papaver somniferum)
entities_mentioned.notes: Opium was used in ancient Egyptian pediatric preparations as a sedative or analgesic. The Bryan translation explicitly names opium, rendering the Egyptian term directly. The designation '(New Style)' may indicate a revised or updated formula within the papyrus tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Fly-dirt-which-is-on-the-Dummy
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fly excrement / fecal deposits from flies (Diptera spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'dummy' refers to an infant's pacifier or soother. Fly excrement scraped from such objects was incorporated into the remedy, possibly as a carrier or on the basis of sympathetic or empirical folk reasoning. This ingredient poses obvious modern sanitary concerns and would be considered hazardous.

results.translator_notes: The parenthetical '(NEW STYLE)' is Bryan's rendering of a phrase in the original papyrus indicating this is a variant or updated version of an older remedy. The segment heading 'DOMESTIC HINTS 163' appears to be a chapter or section reference from Bryan's 1930 editorial organization, not part of the original Egyptian text. The use of opium to sedate infants, while alarming by modern standards, was documented across multiple ancient cultures. The preparation method is not explicitly described beyond the listing of ingredients; the powder form suggests grinding and mixing.
results._label: Remedy To Stop The Crying Of A Child
